text: John chapter number two, and thankful for the opportunity to continue with this study that we have been studying this thought, doing what we ought to do. And of course, I've said it each week, just because we know doesn't mean we do. And this evening, we'll continue or we'll really kind of conclude this particular mini series within the series for the last five weeks. This is part five tonight, we've dealt with this thought of we ought to walk as Christ walked. And it was within the greater theme or series of doing what we ought to do. But we come to first John two and verse number six, and the Bible says, he that sayeth he abideth in him ought himself also so to walk even as he walked. And of course, as we've dealt with this subject now for these last four weeks, tonight being part five, we've discovered several aspects and how with regard to how Christ walked, how did he live during his earthly ministry? I said that first John two, six, really requires us as students of the word to dig into the gospels and find out, well, how did Christ walk? If I'm supposed to walk as Christ walk, then I must know how he walked. And so we looked at a number of things already. And we said that Jesus walked wisely, he walked humbly, he walked purposefully, courageously. Last week, we said that he walked compassionately. And we looked at that truth from the book of Jude and of some have compassion making a difference and how we too are to walk and to live with compassion. I've made this known that as we look at these various aspects of how Christ walked and then incorporating those things in our lives, that it's not that we walk one, they're not individual silos. The reality is that as we walk, as we live our lives, we're to live with compassion and with humility and with purpose and so on and so forth, all at the same time. But we've looked at these as individual lessons and looking at the example tonight, I'm gonna deal with this subject that Jesus walked obediently. Jesus walked obediently. Therefore, of course, we too ought to walk in obedience. And obedience is a subject that we find throughout the Bible and how important it is. John chapter eight, as our manner has been here over these last four weeks, we're going to look at an example from the gospels and then also a reference in the book of Philippians in this particular case of how the Lord Jesus Christ walked obediently. And then as we prove that truth, then we'll go over and begin to look at the truth with regard to how we are to walk in obedience. The reality is I preached this message on Sunday evening with regard to the correction that the church of Ephesus received. And I said this, that when correction comes, each individual has a choice. How are we gonna respond to it? Are we gonna obey it? Are we gonna dismiss it? What are we gonna do? And when we think about the commands that God has given to us, we also recognize the fact that he's given us choice. He's given to us free will. You can obey, you can also disobey. Now, we understand it's in your best interest, it's in my best interest to obey. It goes far better for us when we obey, don't you think? And we see that throughout the scriptures. We see it proven. In John chapter number eight, there's a number of things that are going on here in this particular chapter. We find that Jesus is here and I'm gonna jump, there's no real easy place to jump in, but I wanna look here at beginning in verse number 27. The Bible says, they understood not that he spake to them of the father, then said Jesus unto them, when you have lifted up the son of man, then shall you know that I am he and that I do nothing of myself, but as my father hath taught me, I speak these things. Now, it's interesting because when he would go to the cross if you were to cross-reference and look at other passages of scripture, it's interesting that when they put him on the cross, there were several things that were said. Of course, he was mocked and he was ridiculed, all that he was, the scourging and all that took place. But after the Lord Jesus Christ said it is finished and the veil was rent in twain, there were some soldiers that said, truly, this was the son of God. And they recognized that truth. And so we find that Jesus makes the statement here in verse 28. Notice in verse 29, and he that sent me is with me. The father hath not left me alone for I do always those things that please him. Now, it's interesting as we continue to see here and understand the thought and the truth that Jesus is giving, as he left the glory of heaven, he, remember, he took on himself the form of a servant. He came in human flesh. He never stopped being God, but he was 100% God. He was 100% man. And we find that in that situation, he says, I do always those things that please him. Notice in verse 30, as he spake these words, many believed on him. Then said Jesus to those Jews, which believed on him. Notice what he says to them. If you continue in my word, then are you my disciples indeed. You think about this idea of continuing in my word, it would have and carry with it that thought of living a life of obedience, a life that is consistent with the teachings of God's word. Of course, we find that it's in this chapter as well, in John chapter number eight, that at the end of the chapter, that Jesus is still speaking here to these religious leaders. And notice he says this in verse 51. Verily, verily, I say unto you, if a man keep my saying, he shall never see death. And then said the Jews unto him, now we know that thou hast a devil. Abraham is dead, and the prophets, and thou sayest, if a man keep my saying, he shall never taste of death. Art thou greater than our father Abraham, which is dead, and the prophets are dead, whom makest thou thyself? Oh, if they only knew how much greater he was than Abraham, how much greater he is than Abraham. We find that Jesus answered in verse 54 and said, if I honor myself, my honor is nothing. But I know him, and if I should say I know him not, I shall be a liar like unto you, but I know him and keep his saying. Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day, and he saw it and was glad. Now notice what they say in verse 57. Then said the Jews unto him, thou art not yet 50 years old, and hast thou seen Abraham? And Jesus said unto them, verily, verily, I say unto you, before Abraham was, I am. What a statement of deity. I mean, we find that here, throughout the years, theologians and others have had many things to say about this verse, many that would attack the deity of Christ. They would come to this verse, and they would try to provide so many different angles and aspects with regard to what Jesus was saying, but we understand exactly what he was saying. Furthermore, the Jewish people that he was speaking to absolutely clearly understood what he was saying. He was claiming that he is God. Now, why is he claiming that? Well, because he is, and we find that in verse 59, they knew when he said, before Abraham was, I am, the Bible says in verse 59, then took they up stones to cast at him, but Jesus hid himself and went out of the temple going through the midst of them and so passed by. Why did they wanna throw stones at him? Turn to the book of Philippians, if you will, and I want you to notice what the apostle Paul says in Philippians chapter number two, and this is a passage that is quite just a familiar passage, a powerful passage, and we find that beginning in verse number five of Philippians two, the Bible says, let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus, who being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God, but notice what the Bible says, but made himself of no reputation. Man, how different than so many today that try to make a reputation for themselves. They wanna be known of who they are and what they've accomplished and so on and so forth. Jesus is God, he left the glory of heaven, and he made himself of no reputation. Notice the Bible says, and took upon him the form of a servant and was made in the likeness of men. By the way, this is all necessary to redeem us. Without this truth of the incarnation, without this. truth of Jesus, God in flesh, coming to this earth, mankind could never be redeemed. But we find that Jesus, the son of God, God in flesh, the sinless one, he had to come and he came in this manner. Notice it says in verse number eight, in being found in fashion as a man, notice these statements, he humbled himself. Remember we talked about the very first week that we were looking at this, we dealt with two aspects that first Wednesday night, wisely and humbly, that Jesus walked wisely and humbly and he says he humbled himself. He's God and he humbled himself. So often we find ourselves being prideful and we're fallen man that the only good that we have is the good of God in us. We are what we are by the grace of God. Paul says, I know that in me that is in my flesh well if no good thing, Paul asked this question of the people at Corinth, he says, what do you have that you didn't receive? Well that's a sobering thought, that's a humbling thought, that's a reality check and we find that the Bible says that he humbled himself, notice this, and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross. He became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross. Why was he obedient to go to the cross of Calvary? Well again, because it was for that hour, that was the purpose, the whole reason that Jesus came was to go to the cross and we find that he was obedient. By the way, aren't you thankful that he went to the cross for you? Aren't you thankful that he finished the course, he finished what was laid out for him, he said it is finished and I'm so thankful for that. Now this evening, as we understand that our savior walked in obedience and we understand that he left us an example that we should follow his steps, then we too would say that we as the people of God should walk in obedience, that our lives, my life and your life should be marked by obedience to God's precious word. The reality this evening is that we find that throughout the scriptures, there's a premium that is placed on obedience. It's not something to be neglected, it's not something, well, it's not really a big deal. No, God places a premium on obedience and we find that as we study his word that there's so many things that we need to be aware of and so many different passages that we could go to this evening. We're gonna just look at a few but I want you to think of this, as Jesus said, I do always those things that please him. And then I want you to think about this statement in John chapter number two, the first miracle, the marriage at Cana, when Mary says to the servants, whatsoever he saith unto you, do it. It's very interesting when we think about this idea of complete obedience, that we understand that for many of us this evening, we probably have even today, this week, there have been times in which we've been disobedient to a command that God has given to us in his word. We fall short, but we ought to be striving to live a life that is marked by obedience. Notice what the Bible says, look at a few verses of scripture, look at the book of Luke, if you will, in chapter number six, Luke chapter number six, and Brother Mark went to this text of scripture in Sunday school this past Sunday and I had so many thoughts going through my mind as I had already prepared for this lesson and several weeks ago, I actually thought I was gonna teach this whole lesson in one Wednesday, isn't that funny? This is week number five, I prepared the whole thing six weeks ago, thought I was gonna teach it in one week and here we are five weeks later, and but in Luke chapter number six and verse number 46, the Bible says this, what a powerful statement, why call ye me Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say. Isn't that a soul piercing verse of scripture? I want you to think about the centurion that came to Jesus and he came to Jesus and he had a servant that was sick and Jesus said, I'll come to your house, but the centurion said, no, I'm not worthy that you come to my house and he said that he was a man under authority, in other words, he had, being a centurion, he had soldiers that were under his command and here's what he said, he said this, I say to this servant go and he goes, I say to this servant do and he does and so on and so forth and I think about that truth and the gospel of Matthew, Matthew eight deals with that and you begin to think about the truth of that centurion and Jesus mentions the faith of that man and because he understood that Jesus could simply say the words and he could heal a servant, distance didn't matter, it was no problem for Jesus, but I think about that man that was under authority and from a human perspective, he had authority over a group of soldiers and he said come and they come, go and they go, do and they do, well, they obeyed, they understood what it was to obey. I want you to understand this evening that our God is so much greater than a centurion. He's God and he bought you with his blood, he bought me with his blood, he's my savior and here the scripture say, why call ye me Lord, Lord and do not the things which I say? I said this before from the pulpit, the reality is that many people today are looking for a savior, but they're not looking for a master. They understand perhaps their need for salvation, they understand that without Christ, they're gonna spend eternity in the lake of fire, that they cannot atone for their own sin, but as far as having a Lord, a master in their life, no, that's already occupied by them. They're already gonna take that position, there's no vacancy, if you will, for that office, but the reality is that if he's my savior, he must also be my Lord. I believe it was D.L. Moody said, if he's not Lord of all, then he's not Lord at all. What a powerful statement and here we find, I mean, it's a piercing question. Why is it that we would call him Lord, but then not do what he says? The reality is that if we're going to have and live a life that's marked by obedience, then it requires for us to dig into the scriptures to know what God commands of us, what he expects of us. How many of them multiplied wives unto themselves? Maybe if they just wrote down the law like they were supposed to, it would have kept them from doing that which God specifically prohibited them from doing. But we find this evening that for many of God's people, we don't even know what God expects of us to be doing, we're not digging into the Bible, we don't know the commands of scripture and so how is it that we can obey him? It wasn't an issue with hearing, it was an issue with doing and can I tell you for many today, it's not an issue of hearing, it's an issue of doing. Isn't that interesting? God says to Ezekiel, I'm sending you to the house of Israel. It's not a strange people with a strange language. In other words, it's my people. Ezekiel was in Hebrew and so he's going to go to his own people and he says that if I had sent you to a people of a strange language or a strange speech in hard language, that they would have hearkened unto you, according to verse number 6, but the house of Israel, verse number 7, will not hearken unto thee, for they will not hearken unto me, for all the house of Israel are impotent and hard-hearted. Wow. You know, it's almost a situation here in Israel's history. They had started with God. Amen? They started with God. And then they just kind of got off course. And their hearts are so hardened. God had sent prophet after prophet after prophet, but they were just doing their own thing their own way. God says to Ezekiel, if I had sent you to a different people, they would have hearkened. The reality is not in whether it was a great message or not, it's what we do with the message. It's not about hearing some, as it says in verse 32, someone that can play well on an instrument, someone that can wax eloquent, no. We're good at excusing our disobedience, and we've become very good at it, and the reality is that we need to come to the place where we say, I just want to obey the Lord. And this evening, I would say that the counsel that Mary gives to the servants in John chapter number 2 is good counsel for every one of us this evening. Whatsoever He saith unto you, do it. I'm sure that you're familiar, just as I am, with the little catchphrase of the athletic company Nike, just do it. And the reality is that we see how they've used that and so on and so forth, but I think about the truth of God's Word in this verse of Scripture where she says, do it. You know, the reality is that we don't need to pray about obeying. When God has given us a command in His Word, it's not something that we pray about, it's something that we do. We simply obey Him. There are certainly things that we must pray about and we understand what those are, but when it comes to the commands, the obvious commands that God has given to us in His Word, there's no need for us to pray about those things, we simply do them. There's no need for delayed obedience in the life of a believer. We must obey immediately, right away, seizing the opportunity, not missing the opportunity, but obeying the Lord. This evening as we wrap up this mini-series within this greater series of doing what we ought to do, we find here this evening that Jesus, He walked and left us an example and I hope tonight that you could add, and I mentioned this earlier on, it's not an exhaustive list. There's so many other ways as we study through the Gospels that we see how Christ walked and I'm thankful that He walked with courage and with compassion and with honesty and with humility and I'm thankful that He walked there with wisdom and I'm thankful tonight for the example that He gave us of walking in obedience. And tonight I simply want to say this, if we examine our lives, we know what obedience looks like. We know what it is when someone disobeys what we've said. If you have children, no doubt there's been some time in your life of rearing children that you've given them some instruction and they just failed to obey the instruction. And sometimes you look at the children, as I did many times, over the years, and I would say, there's now a consequence for the disobedience. And you kind of look at it and say, you know, it's really just so simple. If you just obey, then you don't have to deal with this. And every time over the years of rearing children and dealing with that, every time I'd say those words, it was like the Holy Spirit of God would say to me, it's so easy, just obey. God's made it plain. He's given to us his word. It's not a problem with hearing. It's not a problem with having the word. It's a problem in our own lives of doing it, of obeying. And this evening, I hope that we could just make a commitment before the Lord that, you know, I'm gonna strive to live a life of obedience. I'm just gonna obey God. No questions asked, no, I'm not gonna take a poll of what the world's doing and what's commonly accepted and comparing myself amongst ourselves. No, I'm just gonna make a determination that whatsoever he saith, I'm just gonna do. I'm just gonna obey God because to obey is better. There's so many other verses that we could look at tonight with regard to the subject of obedience. It's an oft-discussed subject from this pulpit from various passages throughout God's word. Obedience, obedience, obedience. And let these words ring in your heart. Why calleth ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things that I say? Let's pray together. Father, we love you. We thank you for loving us. We thank you for the opportunity that we have to have your word and to know what you expect of us, what you've commanded us. I pray that you'd help us to be obedient, Lord. Help us not to excuse disobedience in our lives. Help us, Lord, to just make that commitment that we're going to live a life of obedience. I pray that, Lord, if you've put your finger on some specific area in our lives tonight, Lord, I pray that you'd help us to deal with that. I pray that you would help us to seek you, to seek to please you, Lord, through all that we say and all that we do. I pray that you'd bless our time of prayer. We thank you for the privilege that we have each Wednesday evening to review updates from our missionaries and to share requests of prayer and to rejoice one with another of answered prayer. And God, I pray that you'd bless our time of prayer tonight. We pray that you'd be honored and glorified through all that's said and done. For it's in Christ's name we pray, amen. All right, anyone need a prayer?
